title: Chopin Etude Op. 10 No. 4
synthesis_text: Really nice work getting through Op. 10 No. 4 — that etude demands so much from you technically, and your pedaling showed real sensitivity today, keeping things clean rather than letting the chromatic runs blur together. For a piece this ferocious, one continuous run-through is a great way to check in, but your next session will reward you if you break it into shorter chunks and drill the trickiest passages — especially any spots where the right-hand chromatic figures feel rushed or uneven under your fingers. Try isolating a four-bar section at a tempo where every note feels controlled, then gradually push it back up to speed. That kind of focused repetition is really where this etude starts to click!

title: Bach Prelude in C Major WTC Book 1
synthesis_text: Nice work playing through the whole prelude! Your pedaling was genuinely the highlight of this session — you're using it with real restraint, which is exactly what Bach calls for here; those arpeggios need air to breathe, not extra sustain blurring them together. For your next session, try slowing down and drilling just four or eight bars at a time rather than running straight through — the magic of this piece lives in the evenness of every single arpeggio figure, and that consistency is something you build phrase by phrase. Pick a spot where the hand position shifts, like around the middle of the piece, and loop it until it feels as natural as the opening. Even a few extra minutes of focused

title: Debussy Clair de Lune
synthesis_text: Your pedaling is really singing in this session — the way you're letting those harmonies bloom and blend is exactly what Debussy needs, and it's clearly something you've been thinking about. That atmospheric wash of sound is the heart of *Clair de Lune*, so keep trusting your instincts there. Where I'd love to see you focus next is on the long phrases — Debussy writes these beautiful arching melodic lines that need a sense of ebb and flow, like a gentle breathing in and out, and right now they're coming across a bit evenhandedly. Try playing through just the opening *andante* section and ask yourself where each phrase is reaching toward, then let the tempo gently expand and contract around that peak. Since you're running through the whole piece each time, it might also be worth carving out a few minutes to linger on a single section and really sculpt those phrases — that kind of focused work will make the full run-throughs feel much more connected.

title: Bach Prelude in C Major WTC Book 1
synthesis_text: Really nice work today on the pedaling — in this prelude, that's one of the trickiest things to get right. Those arpeggiated figures can so easily turn muddy if you hold the sustain too long, and you're clearly listening carefully to let the harmony breathe. That's a real musical instinct showing through. Since this was a quick run-through, I'd love to see you go back and slow down a few of those arpeggiated figures in the middle section, really feeling each hand shape before you let it flow again — Bach rewards that kind of patient attention. Try isolating just four or five bars at a time and looping them; even five minutes of that kind of focused drilling will do more for you than another full play-through. You're on a good track — now let's dig in a little deeper!

title: Beethoven Moonlight Sonata 1st Movement
synthesis_text:
Your pedaling really sang today — and for the Moonlight Sonata, that's everything. That hazy, nocturnal atmosphere Beethoven is after lives entirely in how you handle the sustain, and you were clearly listening carefully to the resonance you were creating. For a piece this atmospheric, that instinct is a real strength.

Since you played through the whole movement in one go, your next step is to slow down and *linger* in a few specific places — especially those long, rolling triplet passages in the middle section where the melody needs to float above without getting swallowed. Try isolating just a phrase or two and really sculpting the balance between the bass, the triplets, and that singing top voice. Even five minutes of that kind of focused work will unlock a lot. You're building something lovely here — keep going!
